coredata.dc:title: Selective determination of ellagic acid in aqueous solution using blue-green emissive copper nanoclusters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Minglu
Zhang, Yi
Cai, Zhi-feng
coredata.dc:description: Development of beneficial sensors to analyze ellagic acid concentrations is of great importance for food safety and human health. Herein, a facile and fast fluorescent probe was carried out for the excellently selective and sensitive measurement of ellagic acid in real samples through histidine protected copper nanoclusters (histidine@Cu NCs) as a nanosensor. This as-developed histidine@Cu NCs were performed through UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime analysis. The TEM image revealed that this nanomaterial had spherical features with the average diameter of 2.5 ± 0.05 nm. The blue-green fluorescence of this Cu NCs was found under the UV light. Meanwhile, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength were located at 387 nm and 488 nm. After addition of ellagic acid, the fluorescence of histidine@Cu NCs was slowly weakened with excellent linear range of 0.5–300 μM and detection limit of 0.077 μM. The fluorescence weakening mechanism of this nanosensor were attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. Finally, this as-established analysis platform was successfully employed to measure ellagic acid in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Histidine
Ellagic acid
Inner filter effect
Static quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ellagic acid using histidine@Cu NCs as a fluorescent nanosensor
Possible mechanism of histidine@Cu NCs for ellagic acid
Detection of ellagic acid in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The UV–vis absorption spectra of histidine@Cu NCs, CuCl2, ascorbic acid and histidine.
The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of histidine@Cu NCs (inset: the picture under sunlight and UV light).
The TEM image (A) and particle size distribution (B) of histidine@Cu NCs.
(A) The XPS spectra of histidine@Cu NCs; The XPS single spectrum of C1s (B), O1s (C), N1s (D) and Cu2p (E).

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of detection system (inset: the picture of detection system without (a) and with (b) ellagic acid); (B) The linear fit curve between ln(F0/F) value and ellagic acid concentration (0.5–300 μM).

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of ellagic acid, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of histidine@Cu NCs; (B) The fluorescence lifetime of histidine@Cu NCs without and with ellagic acid; (C) The fluorescence quenching efficiency of histidine@Cu NCs by ellagic acid.
Illustration of the preparation of histidine@Cu NCs and the determination of ellagic acid based on histidine@Cu NCs.
Comparison of different analytical methods for ellagic acid measurement.
The ellagic acid detection in real samples.

head.ce:title: Selective determination of ellagic acid in aqueous solution using blue-green emissive copper nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang, 441053, Hubei Province, P.R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang, 441053, Hubei Province, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, P.R. China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Development of beneficial sensors to analyze ellagic acid concentrations is of great importance for food safety and human health. Herein, a facile and fast fluorescent probe was carried out for the excellently selective and sensitive measurement of ellagic acid in real samples through histidine protected copper nanoclusters (histidine@Cu NCs) as a nanosensor. This as-developed histidine@Cu NCs were performed through UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and fluorescence lifetime analysis. The TEM image revealed that this nanomaterial had spherical features with the average diameter of 2.5 ± 0.05 nm. The blue-green fluorescence of this Cu NCs was found under the UV light. Meanwhile, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength were located at 387 nm and 488 nm. After addition of ellagic acid, the fluorescence of histidine@Cu NCs was slowly weakened with excellent linear range of 0.5–300 μM and detection limit of 0.077 μM. The fluorescence weakening mechanism of this nanosensor were attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching. Finally, this as-established analysis platform was successfully employed to measure ellagic acid in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Ellagic acid (C14H6O8) (Fig. S1) possesses a flat structure, which consists of connecting two lactone rings and two benzene rings including two carbonyl groups and four phenolic hydroxyl groups. As a polyphenol dilactone, ellagic acid is widely found in various soft fruits, nuts and other plant tissues [1,2]. As far as we know, ellagic acid has various biologically active functions, such as antioxidant function, anticancer, antimutagenic properties, and inhibitory effect on human immunodeficiency virus [3]. In addition, ellagic acid is also an effective coagulant, which has a good inhibitory effect on a variety of bacteria and viruses, and can protect the wound from the invasion of bacteria, prevent infection, and inhibit ulcers [4]. At the same time, some studies have found that ellagic acid also has antihypertensive and sedative effects [5–7]. The antioxidant effect of ellagic acid makes it widely used in food, medical and pharmaceutical fields. In developed countries such as the United States, ellagic acid is a best-selling bidirectional genetically modified anti-cancer health product, and the price of the product in the international market is very high. However, measurement of ellagic acid concentration in cosmetics on the market is necessary due to its amount has been firmly controlled. For example, the amount of ellagic acid should be lower than 0.5 % (w/w) in cosmetics (the Department of Health, Taiwan) [8]. In view of the importance of ellagic acid, the sensing of ellagic acid has attracted significant interest in recent years. Some techniques have been employed to satisfy the need for the measurement of ellagic acid including electrochemical strategy [8–11], capillary electrophoresis [12], localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [13], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14–17], UV spectrometry [16] and fluorescence method [18–21]. However, these reported technologies received a few restrictions including long time for whole process, multi-procedure preparation process, high cost, lower sensitivity and poor anti-influence ability. Then, the requirement for a facile, fast, appropriate and selective measurement of ellagic acid in real samples is of great importance.

Among them, fluorescence methods have drawn widespread attention on account of their excellent sensitivity, high selectivity, operating simpleness, inexpensive and portability [21–23]. The fluorescence based sensing system through organic dyes, photoproteins, semiconductor quantum dots, carbon-based quantum dots and metal nanoclusters have obtained widespread interest owing to their quick response, excellent sensitivity and visual analysis [24–27]. Regrettably, the before-mentioned methods had complicated preparation steps and poor photostability, while the applications of those was restricted because of critical environmental and health problems resulted from organic dyes and transition metals. Then, the fluorescent measurement nanomaterials could endure the above shortcomings with excellent sensitivity.

Recently, metal nanoclusters (M NCs), as a new class of nanomaterial, have drawn exhibited immense attention in the area of chemo-sensors, bio-sensors, cell-imaging and catalysis applications owing to their strong fluorescence, broad excitation spectra, low toxicity, excellent water solubility and high stability [28–30]. M NCs consist of several to dozens of metal atoms and some protectants and they have different electronics, optics, physics and chemistry characteristics than larger metal nanoparticles. In the past ten years, silver nanoclusters (Ag NCs) and gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) are the first to be noticed and several interdisciplinary applications have been developed using them as fluorescent sensors. Compared with Ag NCs and Au NCs, copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) have attracted immense potential owing to their inexpensive, abundant resources and excellent photochemical features, which are comparable to that of Ag and Au [31,32]. Therefore, the establishment of Cu NCs for drugs detection is still our main research work in the future.

The inner-filter effect (IFE) is frequently treated as a mistake in fluorescent analysis. Recently, owing to its briefness, agility and essentially increased sensitivity and selectivity, IFE has attracted immense interest in probe areas [33,34]. The IFE can be caused by an obvious phenomenon, which is the overlapping between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the detected substances and the fluorescent excitation or emission spectrum of the probes. The development of IFE based probes are agile and it doesn’t need any modification on the M NCs.

In this paper, a selective and sensitive sensor for ellagic acid measurement was developed using blue-green fluorescent Cu NCs, which were prepared through histidine as the protective agent and ascorbic acid as the reducing agent (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescent intensities of as-developed histidine@Cu NCs were visibly quenched by ellagic acid, which was ascribed to the IFE and static quenching mechanism. Due to the fluorescent quenching of histidine@Cu NCs by ellagic acid, the ellagic acid contents were measured linearly through the linear experiments. Finally, this developed system was successfully carried out to measure ellagic acid in pharmaceutical and water samples.

CaCl2, Zn(NO3)2, AlCl3, MgCl2, PbCl2, AgNO3, BaCl2, CdCl2, CrCl3, CuCl2, KCl, NaCl, NiCl2, glycine, histidine, cysteine, glutamic acid, glycine, threonine, tyrosine, glucose, glutathione, alanine, ellagic acid and ascorbic acid were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company (Shanghai, China) without any purification.

The UV–vis absorption spectral analysis were performed through UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a 1 cm × 1 cm UV cuvette. The fluorescence spectra of histidine@Cu NCs were measured on an F-7000 spectrofluorimeter (Hitachi, Japan) under the excitation and emission slits of 5/10 nm with a 1 cm × 1 cm fluorescence cuvette. The transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100) was employed to discuss the morphology and sizes of histidine@Cu NCs at the voltage of 200 kV. The appropriate ellagic acid concentrations were dropped onto the carbon-coated copper grid and dried for the TEM analysis. A Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectroscope was used to perform the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) data of histidine@Cu NCs with the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250) profiles were applied to record the element species and element valence of histidine@Cu NCs. The fluorescence lifetime data were studied by using the Fluo Time 100 (PicoQuant, Germany).

The histidine@Cu NCs were developed based on the related literature with minor modification [35]. Briefly, The histidine solution (20 mL, 0.1 M), CuCl2 solution (0.1 mL, 0.1 M) and ascorbic acid solution (1 mL, 0.1 M) were successively introduced into the reaction flask with stirring for 5 min. Then, the mixture were reacted under 40 ℃ for 2 h. The obtained brown solution was dialyzed via a dialysis bag. Then the solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

Under optimized measurement conditions, different ellagic acid contents were introduced into the histidine@Cu NCs (1 mL) and PBS (1 mL, pH = 8.0) mixture and reacted thoroughly for 1.0 min at room temperature. Then the mixture was dropped into the fluorescence cuvette and the fluorescent data were obtained for the study of the ellagic acid determination.

The quantum yield (QY) of Cu NCs was obtained in reference to Quinine sulfate. The quantum yield of Cu NCs was calculated using equation.
ϕ x =
I x
I s
×
A s
A x
×
η
x
2
η
s
2
×
ϕ s

Where ϕ, A, I, and η expressed the quantum yield, absorbance, integral area of target fluorescence intensity and refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscript s and × indicated the reference and object, respectively.

The as-prepared histidine@Cu NCs were performed through UV–vis absorption, fluorescence, TEM, FT-IR, XPS and fluorescence lifetime. As revealed in Fig. 1 , no any peaks varying from 400 nm to 600 nm were obtained in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of histidine@Cu NCs, indicating that the histidine@Cu NCs did not have larger copper nanoparticles [36,37]. In addition, compared with histidine, ascorbic acid and CuCl2, a distinct absorption peak at 350 nm was displayed, which should be attributed to the formation of histidine and copper atoms. As suggested in Fig. 2 , the maximum emission wavelength of histidine@Cu NCs was located at 488 nm under the excitation wavelength of 387 nm. Blue-green fluorescence of histidine@Cu NCs solution was observed under the UV lamp, and it displayed light brown under sunlight (inset in Fig. 2). The emission features of histidine@Cu NCs were studied under different excitation varying from 365 to 400 nm. As exhibited in Fig. S2, the fluorescent emission wavelength and height were not a fixed value, revealing that the histidine@Cu NCs had excitation-dependent features. The above behaviors were ascribed to the particle size distribution and structural features of histidine@Cu NCs in aqueous solution [38,39]. The TEM image of histidine@Cu NCs was displayed in Fig. 3 A and indicated that its morphology and average particle size were spherical shape and 2.5 ± 0.05 nm, respectively (Fig. 3B).

The FT-IR spectra of histidine@Cu NCs and histidine were suggested in Fig. S3. The peaks at 3411.08 and 3129.39 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of amino and hydroxyl groups [40]. The peaks at 2937.07 and 2892.38 cm−1 were attributed to the CH bond stretching vibration whereas CO bond stretching vibration was located at 1634.75 cm−1 [41]. Additionally, the CN bond stretching vibrations were located at 1144.78 cm−1 [42,43]. The peaks at 1476.74 and 1341.09 cm−1 were the asymmetric and symmetric deformation vibration of CH bond, respectively. The XPS measurement (Fig. 4 A) exhibited that C1s (285.03 eV), N1s (397.66 eV), O1s (531.88 eV) and Cu2p (934.83 eV) were the main elements of histidine@Cu NCs [44]. The single XPS spectrum of C 1 s was divided into three peaks, which were CC bond (285.1 eV), CN bond (286.3 eV) and O-CO (287.9 eV), respectively (Fig. 4B) [45]. The O1s single XPS spectrum (Fig. 4C) suggested two peaks around 530.4 and 531.4 eV, which should be ascribed to CO and OH groups, respectively [46–48]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4D) split into three peaks around 398.0, 399.1 and 400.3 eV, which could be assumed to CN-C, CNC and NH groups, respectively [49,50]. Overall, these XPS data indicated that the Cu NCs produced here were equipped with histidine. The single XPS spectrum of Cu 2p was divided into two peaks, which were Cu2p1/2 (951.88 eV) and Cu2p3/2 (932.78 eV), respectively (Fig. 4F) [45]. The binding energy position for Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 indicated that Cu NCs were composed of Cu0 and Cu+. The fluorescence quantum yield of histidine@Cu NCs was 3.44 %.

The influence of NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensity of histidine@Cu NCs was also suggested at room temperature. As exhibited in Fig. S4A, the fluorescence intensities of histidine@Cu NCs revealed significant unchange according to the change of NaCl concentration. The UV light stability of histidine@Cu NCs was further discussed under different exposure time, and the FL intensities plot was revealed in Fig. S4B. No apparent change was suggested, indicating the excellent anti-ultraviolet interference ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ellagic acid using histidine@Cu NCs as a fluorescent nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: This as-developed histidine@Cu NCs revealed excellent water solubility owing to the existence of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of histidine@Cu NCs. The above feature benefited the application of histidine@Cu NCs for ellagic acid measurement in aqueous solution. The optimal detection time and pH value were 1.0 min and 8.0, respectively (Fig. S5). The fluorescent spectra of histidine@Cu NCs with different ellagic acid contents were suggested in Fig. 5A. Before addition of ellagic acid, the fluorescent intensity reached the maximum value at the emission wavelength of 488 nm. With increasing the ellagic acid concentrations from 0 to 300 μM, the fluorescent intensities of this system at 488 nm were gradually quenched. When the ellagic acid concentrations were added into 300 μM, the fluorescence quenching rate could reach 90.5 %. As displayed in Fig. 5B, a linear fit curve was obtained by taking ln(F0/F) as the ordinate and ellagic acid concentration (0.5–300 μM) as the abscissa. The linear fit formula was ln(F0/F) = 0.0077[Q] + 0.0875 with R2 of 0.9936 (inset Fig. 5 B). The fluorescence image of histidine@Cu NCs were compared with and without ellagic acid under UV lamp (inset Fig. 5A). The comparison of linear range and detection limit in different methods were listed in Table 1 . Obviously, this as-developed nanosensor had wider linear range or lower detection limit. The results suggested that this nanoprobe could rapidly measure ellagic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of histidine@Cu NCs for ellagic acid
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, the fluorescent weakening mechanism of probe with different detected substances were listed: fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching and dynamic quenching [51,52]. To clarify the mechanism of histidine@Cu NCs for ellagic acid detection, the optical features of histidine@Cu NCs and ellagic acid were systematically studied.

The relationship between UV–vis absorption spectrum of ellagic acid and fluorescence excitation spectrum of histidine@Cu NCs were studied and displayed in Fig. 6 A. As shown in Fig. 6A, an obvious overlapping feature between the UV absorption spectrum of ellagic acid and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of histidine@Cu NCs. The results should be attributed to FRET or IFE [53]. Furthermore, this judgment could be carried out through the fluorescence lifetime of histidine@Cu NCs with and without ellagic acid. The fluorescence lifetime spectra were revealed in Fig. 6B. According to the relevant calculation, the average lifetimes of histidine@Cu NCs without and with ellagic acid were 4.28 ns (t1 = 2.56 ns, t2 = 6.29 ns, B1 = 1417.75, B2 = 496.21) and 4.12 ns (t1 = 1.91 ns, t2 = 5.24 ns, B1 = 588.06, B2 = 422.42), respectively. The difference of fluorescence lifetime revealed that the FRET was ruled out. To further discuss the above presumption, the observed intensities were corrected through the formula (1) [54,55].
(1)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em
where Fcor and Fobsd refer to the corrected (removing IFE) and observed fluorescence intensities. Aex and Aem are the absorbance at the excitation (387 nm) and emission (488 nm) wavelengths, respectively. In addition, the value of g, s and d are 0.25, 0.50 and 1.0 cm, respectively. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (E%) of the observed and corrected of histidine@Cu NCs with ellagic acid were displayed in Fig. 6C. The observed value was larger than corrected value, revealing the mechanism should be the primary IFE [56].

Moreover, the coexistence of static quenching or dynamic quenching was also potential. Thus, the Stern-Volmer equation (2) was applied to study the static quenching or dynamic quenching [57,58].
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of histidine@Cu NCs without and with ellagic acid, respectively. K SV refers to the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. [Q] refers to the ellagic acid contents. τ 0 refers to the fluorescence lifetime of histidine@Cu NCs (4.28 ns). Herein, a linear curve was obtained through F0/F value as the ordinate and ellagic acid content as the abscissa, and the linear equation was F0/F = 0.0136[Q] + 0.9899 (R2 = 0.9977) (observed fluorescence intensity) (Fig. 6D). In the light of this linear equation, K SV and k q were carried out to be 1.36 × 104 L·mol−1 and k q value of 3.18 × 1012 L·mol−1·s−1. The k q value was significantly greater than the maximum scatter collision quenching constant (2 × 1010 L·mol−1·s−1), revealing that the other mechanism was static quenching [59,60].

Selectivity is a crucial element to assess the property of a fluorescence nanoprobe. To assess the selectivity of this prepared system, the effect of different substances (such as CaCl2, Zn(NO3)2, AlCl3, MgCl2, PbCl2, AgNO3, BaCl2, CdCl2, CrCl3, CuCl2, KCl, NaCl, NiCl2, glycine, histidine, cysteine, glutamic, leucine, threonine, tyrosine, glucose, glutathione, alanine, ellagic acid and ascorbic acid) on the fluorescent intensity of histidine@Cu NCs was discussed. Fig. S6A displayed the fluorescent intensities of histidine@Cu NCs with addition of 10 times contents interfering compounds (3000 μM) than ellagic acid (300 μM). It was distinct from Fig. S6A that the fluorescent intensities around 488 nm were not obviously changed with the addition of control substances. The results clearly verified that this proposed nanoprobe could easily distinguish ellagic acid among other control substances. Additionally, the images of histidine@Cu NCs with different test substances under UV light were displayed in Fig. S6B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ellagic acid in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further evaluate the possible actual applications of this developed nanosensor, the detection of ellagic acid in real samples were performed. The medical glucose samples were purchased from hospital. The river water samples were obtained from campus. The recovery measurements were implemented on the samples added with different ellagic acid concentrations (15, 35, 55 μM) through this proposed platform. This addition system was used to measure the recoveries of ellagic acid medical glucose and water samples. As listed in Table 2 , this as-prepared system displayed justifiable recoveries with range of 95.9–106.5 % and revealed excellent reproducibility and accuracy with the RSD of 2.78–3.46 %. The results revealed that this as-developed nanosensor had huge potential for ellagic acid determination in actual samples.

In summary, a fast and excellently sensitive system was carried out to measure ellagic acid in real samples by using this as-developed blue-green fluorescent histidine@Cu NCs. The fluorescent intensities of histidine@Cu NCs were weakened after the addition of ellagic acid. According to the fluorescent repression, the ellagic acid contents were measured. The detection linear range and limit were 0.5–300 μM and 0.077 μM, respectively. The IFE and static quenching effect were the main fluorescent weakening mechanisms of histidine@Cu NCs by ellagic acid. The prepared detection platform also had good selectivity even with other control substances. This fluorescent technique was smoothly used for ellagic acid determination in medical glucose and river water samples with satisfactory recoveries and RSD. This IFE-based method developed here could forge a new path for the simple and fast determination of ellagic acid through histidine@Cu NCs, since this method prevented complicated preparation of sensor.
Minglu Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Yi Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank the Doctoral Startup Foundation of Hubei University of Arts and Science (No.2059135), the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province of China (No.2022CFD099) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122597.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ellagic acid attenuates liver toxicity induced by valproic acid in rats

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.F. Abdelkader, M. Elyamany, A.M. Gad, N. Assaf, H.M. Fawzy, W.H. Elesawy, Ellagic acid attenuates liver toxicity induced by valproic acid in rats, J. Pharmacol. Sci. 143 (2020) 23-29.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Physical formulation approaches for improving aqueous solubility and bioavailability of ellagic acid: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Nyamba, A. Lechanteur, R. Semdé, B. Evrard, Physical formulation approaches for improving aqueous solubility and bioavailability of ellagic acid: A review, Eur. J. Pharm. Biopharm. 159 (2021) 198-210.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ameliorative role of ellagic acid against acute liver steatosis in adult zebrafish experimental model

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Venkatasubramanian, A. Thiyagaraj, V. Subbiah, S. Solairaja, S. Arumugam, S. Ramalingam, S. Venkatabalasubramanian, Ameliorative role of ellagic acid against acute liver steatosis in adult zebrafish experimental model, Comp. Biochem. Phys. C 247 (2021) 109061.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of a supersaturatable self-microemulsion as drug delivery system for ellagic acid and evaluation of its antioxidant activities

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.D. Zheng, C.M. Lv, X. Sun, J.L. Wang, Z.X. Zhao, Preparation of a supersaturatable self-microemulsion as drug delivery system for ellagic acid and evaluation of its antioxidant activities, J. Drug Deliv. Sci. Tec. 53 (2019) 101209.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ellagic acid prevents myocardial infarction-induced left ventricular diastolic dysfunction in ovariectomized rats

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.M. Costa, V. Mengal, G.A. Brasil, A.A. Peluso, J.T. Treebak, P.W. Endlich, S.A. de Almeida, G.R. de Abreu, Ellagic acid prevents myocardial infarction-induced left ventricular diastolic dysfunction in ovariectomized rats, J. Nutr. Biochem. 105 (2022) 108990.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ellagic acid can act as a chaperone and suppress the heat-induced amyloid-like aggregation of ovalbumin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.J. Yan, Z. Zhou, Ellagic acid can act as a chaperone and suppress the heat-induced amyloid-like aggregation of ovalbumin, Food Hydrocolloid. 100 (2020) 105408.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Volatile constituents and ellagic acid formation in strawberry fruits of selected cultivars

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhao, J.J. Liu, F.Q. Wang, S.S. Wang, H. Feng, X.B. Xie, F.L. Hao, L.Z. Zhang, C.B. Fang, Volatile constituents and ellagic acid formation in strawberry fruits of selected cultivars, Food Res. Int. 138 (2020) 109767.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantification of ellagic acid in cosmetic products by using a partially preanodized screen-printed carbon electrode coupled with flow injection analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.Y. Chen, Y.M. Ji, C.H. Luo, Y.S. Chen, Y. Shih, Quantification of ellagic acid in cosmetic products by using a partially preanodized screen-printed carbon electrode coupled with flow injection analysis, Anal. Methods 3 (2011) 205-209.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of ellagic acid in strawberries, raspberries and blackberries by square-wave voltammetry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Komorsky-Lovric, I. Novak, Determination of ellagic acid in strawberries, raspberries and blackberries by square-wave voltammetry, Int. J. Electrochem. Sci. 6 (2011) 4638-4647.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.S. Kumar, Y.M. Ji, S. Sornambikai, P.Y. Chen, Y. Shih, Flow injection analysis of ellagic acid in cosmetic skin-whitening creams using a dendritic nanostructured copper-gold alloy plated screen-printed carbon electrode, Int. J. Electrochem. Sci. 6 (2011) 5344-5356.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltammetric behaviour and square-wave voltammetric determination of the potent antioxidant and anticarcinogenic agent ellagic acid in foodstuffs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Cuartero, J.A. Ortuno, P. Truchado, M.S. García, F.A. Tomas-Barberan, M.I. Albero, Voltammetric behaviour and square-wave voltammetric determination of the potent antioxidant and anticarcinogenic agent ellagic acid in foodstuffs, Food Chem. 128 (2011) 549-554.
